summary.narrative: Mayor Adams has made measurable progress on housing initiatives, exceeding his 2024 affordable housing goal and advancing ambitious long-term housing plans. However, his accelerated supportive housing timeline appears unmet, and several major promises remain works in progress. His administration has followed through on education and budget-related commitments with mixed results.

promises.text: Advance 24 affordable housing projects on public land in 2024
promises.evidence: The Adams administration announced it surpassed this commitment, advancing 26 affordable housing projects on public land in 2024, exceeding the goal by two projects.
The administration has released ambitious plans including the Manhattan Plan targeting 100,000 new homes over a decade. Progress is being made but the 2032 deadline is still years away.

promises.text: Complete 15,000 units of supportive housing in 10 years (by 2025) instead of 15 years
promises.evidence: Adams promised to accelerate de Blasio's 15/15 Supportive Housing Initiative to completion by 2025. There is no evidence this accelerated timeline was met.

promises.text: Bring back the gifted and talented school program
promises.evidence: Adams reversed the previous administration's plan to phase out the Gifted and Talented program and expanded it, restoring the screening process for admissions.

promises.text: Implement agency budget cuts ranging from 3% to 5%
promises.evidence: Adams implemented multiple rounds of agency budget cuts known as Programs to Eliminate the Gap (PEGs), requiring city agencies to find savings in the 3-5% range.

promises.text: Improve relations with New York State government
promises.evidence: Adams has worked with Albany on housing legislation and other initiatives, though tensions have emerged over migrant funding and other issues. Relations remain mixed.

promises.text: Review property taxes for potential reform
promises.evidence: Adams committed to reviewing the city's complex property tax system. Some advisory work has been done, but comprehensive reform requires state approval and remains incomplete.

promises.text: Fight harder for New York families than anyone else
promises.evidence: This broad rhetorical promise is difficult to measure objectively. Adams has pursued various quality-of-life initiatives, though his administration faces federal corruption charges that have complicated his tenure.
